content.problemStatement:
Your task is to write a program to


Remove the extra spaces at the start and end of the string using strip().
Split the cleaned string into words using split().
Convert the list of words into a tuple and print the number of unique words in the tuple.

Input Format
A single line containing the sentence (string with words separated by spaces, may have leading/trailing spaces).



Output Format
Print the count of unique words in the tuple.
constraints: Words contain only printable non‑space characters
Total number of words in a sentence ≤ 20
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a sentence consisting of words separated by spaces. Sentences may have leading or trailing spaces and may contain multiple consecutive spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the number of distinct words in the sentence after removing leading/trailing spaces and splitting by whitespace.

sampleTestCases.input: 2\nhello world hello python\ndata science data data AI AI
sampleTestCases.explanation: First sentence after stripping and splitting gives words [hello, world, hello, python]; distinct words are {hello, world, python} → 3. Second sentence yields [data, science, data, data, AI, AI]; distinct words are {data, science, AI} → 3.

Test case 1: after stripping we have \"single\" → 1 unique word. Test case 2: line contains only spaces → no words → 0. Test case 3: words are [alpha, beta, gamma, alpha, beta]; distinct set {alpha, beta, gamma} → 3.
Words are case‑sensitive, so \"Python\", \"python\" and \"PYTHON\" are three different strings.

solution.approach: For each test case we first strip leading and trailing whitespace from the line. Then we split the cleaned line on one or more whitespace characters to obtain the list of words. By inserting each word into a hash set (or using a set in Python) we automatically keep only distinct words. The size of the set is the required answer. This works in linear time with respect to the number of characters in the line and uses O(k) extra memory where k is the number of distinct words (≤ 20).
solution.timeComplexity: O(total number of characters across all test cases) – each character is examined a constant number of times.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(k) per test case, where k ≤ 20 is the number of distinct words.
